lesson.title: Displaying Data
lesson.body: Different data displays serve different purposes. A **dot plot** shows each value as a dot — good for small datasets. A **stem-and-leaf plot** splits each value into a stem (leading digit) and leaf (last digit) — preserves actual values. A **histogram** groups data into intervals (bars, no gaps) — good for large datasets and seeing distribution shape. A **box plot** shows median, quartiles, and range — best for comparing distributions. Choose the display that best fits the data and question.

rooms.question: A stem-and-leaf plot shows: 3 | 2 5 8 and 4 | 1 7. What is the median of this data?
rooms.explanation: The stem-and-leaf gives: 32, 35, 38, 41, 47. Five values; the middle (3rd) is 38. The 35 answer picks the 2nd value. The 37 is the mean ≈ (32+35+38+41+47)/5 = 193/5 = 38.6 ≈ 37... actually that's not right either — mean = 193/5 = 38.6. The median is 38.

rooms.question: A histogram has bars at: [0–10): height 3, [10–20): height 7, [20–30): height 5. How many data values are in the dataset?
rooms.explanation: Each bar's height represents the count (frequency) of values in that interval. Total count = 3 + 7 + 5 = 15 data values. The 5 error counts bars instead of summing frequencies.

rooms.question: Which display is best for comparing the spread of test scores between two different classes?
rooms.explanation: Side-by-side box plots directly compare medians (the line inside the box), the interquartile range (box width), and overall range (whiskers) between two groups. Dot plots work but become cluttered. A stem-and-leaf can only show two groups with a back-to-back plot. A single histogram can't show two groups.

rooms.question: In a box plot, the box spans from Q1 = 15 to Q3 = 35. What is the Interquartile Range (IQR)?
rooms.explanation: IQR = Q3 − Q1 = 35 − 15 = 20. The IQR represents the middle 50% of the data. A small IQR means the middle half is tightly clustered; a large IQR means it's spread out. The range (50) uses minimum and maximum, not quartiles.

rooms.question: Order these data displays from most to least useful for showing the exact original data values:
rooms.explanation: Dot plot shows every individual value. Stem-and-leaf preserves every value in a compact form. Histogram groups values into bins (detail lost). Box plot only shows 5 numbers (most detail lost). The question is: which preserves the most individual values?

boss.name: The Chart Specter
boss.taunt: I have charted every outcome. All end badly.

questions.question: A box plot has: min=10, Q1=25, median=40, Q3=55, max=80. What percent of data lies between 25 and 55?
questions.explanation: By definition, Q1 to Q3 contains the middle 50% of the data. Q1 = 25th percentile and Q3 = 75th percentile — the box spans 50% of the data. The 25% answer gives only one quartile's worth.

questions.question: A histogram shows most data clustered near 60–80, with a long tail toward lower values. This distribution is:
questions.explanation: A long tail toward lower values = skewed left (negatively skewed). 'Skewed' is named after the direction of the tail, not the bulk of the data. Most values are high (60–80), but a few low outliers pull the tail left.

questions.question: Test scores: 55, 60, 65, 70, 70, 75, 80, 90, 95, 100. The data is displayed in a histogram with interval width 10. How many bars have height > 1?
